perturbation.original_text: The Data Protection Act 1998 reiterates this point and makes it a legal requirement that effective agreements exist where a third party processes data.
perturbation.changed_text: The Data Protection Act 1998 suggests this point and makes it a recommendation that agreements exist where a third party processes data.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'makes it a legal requirement' to 'makes it a recommendation' weakens the legally binding nature of data protection agreements, contradicting the actual requirements of the Data Protection Act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Data Protection Act 2018 (which supersedes the 1998 act but maintains similar principles)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 121 of the Data Protection Act 2018
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts the Data Protection Act 2018 (which replaced the 1998 act) by weakening the mandatory requirement for data protection agreements when third parties process data. Section 121 deals with processing in compliance with the GDPR and therefore still requires effective agreements.

perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Data Protection Act 1998 'makes it a legal requirement' for effective agreements. The changed text weakens this to 'makes it a recommendation'. This directly contradicts the initial legally binding nature established by the original contract language. The Law Explanation states that the Data Protection Act 2018 (which replaced the 1998 act) still requires effective agreements and therefore the change contradicts this. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces the existence of the Data Protection Act 2018. The change from 'legal requirement' to 'recommendation' significantly weakens the obligation, making it likely unenforceable, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Any patient information, particularly their health or treatment details, is highly sensitive. If such information was leaked to the wrong people, it could cause s evere embarrassment to the patients concerned, their families and to the organisation. There could be legal action following such a breach of confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: Any patient information, particularly their health or treatment details, is considered moderately sensitive. If such information was leaked, it could cause slight inconvenience to the patients. No legal action can be taken.
perturbation.explanation: This change drastically downplays the sensitivity of patient information and removes the possibility of legal action, contradicting legal protections afforded to patient data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) - U.S. Equivalent
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that patient information is only 'moderately sensitive' and that 'no legal action can be taken', the modified text directly contradicts HIPAA regulations which establish strict standards for protecting sensitive patient health information and provide legal avenues for addressing breaches.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: such other person; or (c) the request for access is made by the individual's personal representative and the provision of access to such personal representative is reasonably likely to cause substantial harm to the individual or another person. A covered entity may deny access to individuals, without providing the individual an opportunity for review, in the following protected situations: (a) the protected health information falls under an exception to the right of access; (b) an inmate request for protected health information under certain circumstances; (c) information that a provider creates or obtains in the course of research that includes treatment for which the individual has agreed not to have access as part of consenting to participate in the research (as long as access to the information is restored upon completion of the research); (d) for records subject to the Privacy Act, information to which access may be denied under the Privacy Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552a; and (e) information obtained under a promise of confidentiality from a source other than a health care provider, if granting access would likely reveal the source. 45 C.F.R. § 164.524. 58 45 C.F.R. § 164.526. 59 Covered entities may deny an individual's request for amendment only under specified circumstances. A covered entity may deny the request if it: (a) may exclude the information from access by the individual; (b) did not create the information (unless the individual provides a reasonable basis to believe the originator is no longer available); (c) determines that the information is accurate and complete; or (d) does not hold the information in its designated record set. 164.526(a)(2). 60 45 C.F.R. § 164.528. 61 45 C.F.R. § 164.522(a). 62 45 C.F.R. § 164.522(a). In addition, a restriction agreed to by a covered entity is not effective under this subpart to prevent uses or disclosures permitted or required under §§ 164.502(a)(2)(ii), 164.510(a) or 164.512. 63 45 C.F.R. § 164.522(b). 64 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(i). 65 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(a). 66 45 C.F.R. §160.103. 67 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(b). 68 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(e). 69 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(f). 70 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(c). 71 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(d). 72 45 C.F.R. § 164.520(b)(1)(vi). 73 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(g). 74 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(h). 75 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(j). 76 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(k). 77 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.103, 164.105. 78 45 C.F.R. § 164.103. 79 45 C.F.R. §164.105. Common ownership exists if an entity possesses an ownership or equity interest of five percent or more in another entity; common control exists if an entity has the direct or indirect power significantly to influence or direct the actions or policies of another entity. 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.103. 80 The Privacy Rule at 45 C.F.R. § 160.103 identifies five types of organized health care arrangements: A clinically-integrated setting where individuals typically receive health care from more than one provider. An organized system of health care in which the participating covered entities hold themselves out to the public as part of a joint arrangement and jointly engage in utilization review, quality assessment and improvement activities, or risk-sharing payment activities. A group health plan and the health insurer or HMO that insures the plan's benefits, with respect to protected health information created or received by the insurer or HMO that relates to individuals who are or have been participants or beneficiaries of the group health plan. All group health plans maintained by the same plan sponsor. All group health plans maintained by the same plan sponsor and all health insurers and HMOs that insure the plans' benefits, with respect to protected health information created or received by the insurers or HMOs that relates to individuals who are or have been participants or beneficiaries in the group health plans. 81 45 C.F.R. § 164.506(c)(5). 82 45 C.F.R. § 164.504(g). 83 45 C.F.R. § 164.504(f). 84 45 C.F.R. § 164.502(g). 85 45 C.F.R. §160.203. 86 45 C.F.R. § 160.202.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the legal obligations surrounding patient information, as it diminishes the sensitivity of such data and eliminates the possibility of legal action following a breach. This is in direct opposition to laws such as HIPAA, which impose strict standards for protecting patient health information and provide legal avenues for breaches. Specifically, the original text stated that 'legal action' could follow a breach of confidentiality. The changed text states 'No legal action can be taken'. This creates a direct contradiction. Snippet 1 identifies the eCFR as authoritative, and snippet 2 alludes to 45 C.F.R. § 164.530, which relates to administrative requirements under HIPAA. The change constitutes a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement relates to requirements of the Data Protection Act 1998, the Human Rights Act 1998, the ‘common law duty of confidentiality’ and the Freedom of Information Act (2000).
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement loosely relates to suggestions found in the Data Protection Act 1998, the Human Rights Act 1998, and a vague 'common understanding of discretion'.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the legal foundation of the agreement by replacing 'requirements' with 'suggestions' and removing the Freedom of Information Act reference, creating legal uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the reference to the Freedom of Information Act 2000 and diminishing the binding nature of the agreement, the modified text contradicts the legal obligations imposed by the Act regarding access to information held by public authorities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Freedom of information guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content Freedom of information guidance and resources Find out about your obligations under the Freedom of Information Act and how to respond to requests. We've recently restructured our FOI guidance and resources. Please let us know what you think . Back to top
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the legal foundation of the agreement, creating a contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition. Specifically, changing 'requirements' to 'suggestions' undermines the binding nature of the legal obligations. Furthermore, removing the reference to the Freedom of Information Act 2000, as explained, contradicts obligations regarding access to information. The ICO snippet provided states the need to find out about obligations under the FOI Act and how to respond to requests. The modified agreement omits it, making it non-compliant, especially for public authorities.

perturbation.original_text: Any information (personal or organisational) will only be used for purposes agreed between the organisations.
perturbation.changed_text: Any information (personal or organisational) can be used for any purpose, regardless of prior agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change completely negates the principle of data protection and lawful processing by removing the restriction on using information only for agreed purposes.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change violates Article 5(1)(b) of the GDPR, which states that personal data shall be 'collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: UK GDPR guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content UK GDPR guidance and resources Due to the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025, this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The Plans for new and updated guidance page will tell you about which guidance will be updated and when this will happen. Back to top
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows personal or organizational information to be used for 'any purpose, regardless of prior agreement,' which directly contradicts the principle of data protection. The LLM's explanation highlights that this change violates Article 5(1)(b) of the GDPR, which mandates that personal data be 'collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes'. The scraped law snippet from ICO confirms that UK GDPR guidance is actively maintained. The original text restricted data use to agreed purposes, aligning with data protection principles. The changed text removes this restriction entirely, creating a clear contradiction by allowing data use incompatible with its original purpose.

perturbation.original_text: All personal identifiable information will be treated as confidential and will not be disclosed to any other persons outside the requi rements of the above agreed purpose(s), without agreement of the ‘data controller’. Any organisational information marked as ‘commercial’ or ‘sensitive’ or by implication of the subject could prejudice the commercial interests of either party will be treat ed as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: All personal identifiable information may be disclosed to any person at the contractor's discretion, regardless of any prior agreements. Organizational information is not considered confidential under any circumstance.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the fundamental principle of confidentiality and data protection, allowing for arbitrary disclosure of personal and organizational information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification violates Article 6(1) of the GDPR, which requires that processing of personal data is lawful only if specific conditions are met, such as consent or legitimate interest. Unrestricted disclosure is not a lawful basis for processing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original contract by allowing the unrestricted disclosure of personal and organizational information, while the original text mandates confidentiality. The LLM's law explanation points out a potential violation of GDPR Article 6(1) due to the absence of a lawful basis for processing personal data with unrestricted disclosure. Although no specific law snippets were provided, the fundamental shift from confidentiality to unrestricted disclosure constitutes a serious legal contradiction, aligning with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions of data protection and privacy, making the contract unenforceable.
